summary: Gruber uses a Magic 8-Ball Q&A format to dissect Napster's $30 million Super Bowl ad campaign for its 'Napster To Go' subscription service. He argues that Napster's '$10,000 to fill an iPod' marketing claim is fundamentally dishonest, since almost no one fills their iPod exclusively with purchased music. He points out that $15/month for a subscription actually exceeds what most people spend on music annually, undermining Napster's value proposition. Gruber further argues that Apple could easily launch its own subscription service if the model proved viable, and that the iPod's market dominance creates a self-reinforcing loop that competitors like Napster simply cannot break. The piece concludes that Napster's subscription pivot is essentially an admission of defeat in the pay-per-download market.
key_points: Napster's claim that it costs $10,000 to fill an iPod is misleading because virtually no one purchases all their music from iTMS — most iPod music comes from ripped CDs
At $180/year, Napster's subscription costs more than what most iPod owners actually spend on music, undermining the value argument
Apple's pay-per-download model creates stronger customer lock-in than subscription services, where switching costs are minimal
The iPod's market dominance creates a self-reinforcing loop: iPod popularity drives iTMS success, and iTMS purchases keep users on iPod
Napster's subscription push signals they've conceded the pay-per-download market to Apple
money_quotes: These companies are going out of their way to draw unrealistic comparisons to the iPod and iTunes, and the mainstream media are giving them play not because they really pose a credible threat to Apple, but just because it's an easy way to write a story.
Yes, if you wanted to fill a 40 GB iPod only using songs purchased from iTMS, yes, it would cost $10,000. But it's a ridiculous comparison, because no one is doing that.
$15 a month is $180 per year. That's a lot more than most people spend on music, isn't it?

Apple could easily jump right in and launch their own iTunes subscription service. They'd just make it simpler, and perhaps even cheaper, than everyone else's, and then Steve Jobs would annoy the hell out of the industry by declaring it a 'brand-new' and 'innovative' way to listen to music.

With subscription services, there's very little barrier to switching from one service to another. In short, a popular pay-per-download service seems like a better long-term business than a popular subscription service.
The popularity of the iPod and iTunes has led to the success of the iTunes Music Store; and the more songs people purchase from iTMS, the more likely they are to stick with the iPod and iTunes.
There's nothing that a company like Napster can do that Apple can't match, and there's little Napster can offer to appeal to the customers Apple already has.
key_insight: Napster's subscription marketing is built on a dishonest comparison that ignores how people actually use iPods, and the iPod's dominant market position creates a self-reinforcing ecosystem that subscription competitors cannot penetrate.
